main_summary.summary: William "Willie" S. Leventhal, born in New York in 1946 and raised in Los Angeles, was interviewed on April 13 (year unstated) in Malibu, California, by David Cline for the Southern Oral History Program, the Smithsonian NMAAHC, and the Library of Congress. Leventhal recounts a Jewish upbringing in the Ramona Gardens federal housing project and the South Bay, where exposure to antisemitic slurs and the Jackie Robinson story shaped his early racial consciousness. As a UCLA freshman, he answered Martin Luther King Jr.'s on-campus February 1965 recruiting call for the SCOPE (Summer Community Organization and Political Education) project organized by Hosea Williams. After orientation at Morehouse College alongside Bayard Rustin, A. Philip Randolph, C. Vann Woodward, John Hope Franklin, Septima Clark, Vernon E. Jordan Jr., Charles Morgan Jr., Clarence Mitchell Jr., and John Doar, he was assigned to Bibb County, Georgia, working under William P. Randall in Macon. He was arrested twice in Macon, then arrested with John R. Lewis in Americus while attempting to integrate the First Methodist Church the week after Andrew Whatley was killed. He was shot at twice in Americus, stayed past the project's end for the Reverend Marcus Moon water-commissioner election in Macon, and later served as SCLC Western Bureau college coordinator. He recounts a 112-day Los Angeles County jail incarceration following a Santa Monica gun-purchase incident and conflict with police in fall 1966, during which jail psychiatrist Marcus Crahan diagnosed him as unstable.
key_themes: Hosea Williams's leadership and battle-plan organizing
Americus, Georgia and Andrew Whatley's killing
main_summary.historical_significance:
Leventhal's account preserves an extensively detailed first-person record of SCLC's SCOPE project, a 1965 summer voter-registration effort that recruited 500 of a planned 2,000 volunteers into 90 of 120 targeted counties across the rural South. He documents the orientation week at Morehouse College and the SCLC-SNCC tensions, names specific co-workers across Macon and Americus host families and local leadership (William P. Randall, Tom Jackson, the Simmons family, Sammy Mahone, Gloria Wise), and provides eyewitness testimony to the week Andrew Whatley was killed, including church-integration arrests with John R. Lewis at the hands of Sheriff Fred B. Chappell.

The interview also functions as a sustained critique of how the published civil rights historiography of David J. Garrow and Taylor Branch, in Leventhal's view, weaponized FBI surveillance records and erased SCOPE from the canonical narrative. He details his own work on the federal report to President Reagan and Congress for Coretta Scott King on the first King Holiday under Lloyd Davis, and traces post-1965 SCLC Western Bureau activity (Operation Breadbasket, the Western Christian Leadership Conference under Rev. Thomas Kilgore Jr. and Gwen Green) that has rarely been documented elsewhere.

chapters.title: My Dad Just Got Back From the War
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Malibu in April 2013, Willie Leventhal opens with his roots: born in New York in 1946 to a chiropractor father and a teacher mother who became a USC special-education professor, a family of educators and immigrant shoe-store owners on Staten Island, and the postwar move to California where Hubert Humphrey and the 1948 Democratic convention were his father's lodestars.

chapters.title: Ramona Gardens and East LA
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes living in the Ramona Gardens federal housing project around 1948, his mother working in the campaign of Edward R. Roybal, his grandfather Morris Segal who ran New York's veterans and vocational education, and a childhood mixing Jewish, Hispanic, and Black East LA before the family moved to North Hollywood and then the South Bay after his parents divorced.

chapters.title: The Word Kike and the Jackie Robinson Story
chapters.summary: He recalls being one of few Jewish kids among Catholic ballplayers who used the word kike as a casual slur, the painful confusion of the Christ-killer charge of that era, and seeing the film The Jackie Robinson Story at age five, which fused his love of baseball with his first reckoning with racial prejudice.

chapters.title: He Was a Healer, Not Just a Ballplayer
chapters.summary: Leventhal explains that what fascinated him about Robinson was not athletic skill but that Robinson confronted the people hurting others and healed the wound, recounts his mother forcing his Jewish confirmation at fifteen after he resisted, and names the athletic Jewish role models, Hank Greenberg, Sandy Koufax, Al Rosen, and his friend Mike Vogel, who countered the Woody Allen stereotype.

chapters.title: Dr. King Found Me Before the Peace Corps Did
chapters.summary: He describes John F. Kennedy as a generational hero whose call to service resonated, the Brooklyn Dodgers arriving with three Jewish players, and Dr. King coming to UCLA in 1965 when Leventhal was eighteen to ask for volunteers for the Summer Community Organization and Political Education project, known as SCOPE.

chapters.title: This Is About Voting
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts that before King came, Jackie Robinson had been a larger influence than King, but he followed the news when King and the SCLC entered Selma on January 1st at the request of local educators and leaders like Amelia Boynton, and how he argued with a girlfriend's stepfather that registering to vote in a democracy could not be wrong.

chapters.title: Scared, But Ready to Go
chapters.summary: He tells how King's appeal at UCLA left him wanting to go but afraid, until a Jewish friend he had played football with confronted him, and describes the campus organizers Joel Siegel and Rick Tuttle, his first arrest in Americus, and his relief when the police car turned toward the jail rather than out into the country.

chapters.title: The Three Versions of Love
chapters.summary: Leventhal recalls King recruiting at UCLA in person and the two things that struck him in the speech: King's discussion of the Greek words for love, agape, philia, and eros, and his joke that Baptist preachers do not give short sermons, presenting a soft-spoken, likable man rather than the famous orator.

chapters.title: A Pulitzer I Would Not Want
chapters.summary: He describes Franklin Murphy introducing King as the moral leader of the country while the FBI tried to sabotage every venue, and recounts how a publisher wanted sex material about King added to his manuscript, which he refused, contrasting himself with David Garrow and Taylor Branch, who used Hoover's files and won Pulitzer Prizes.

chapters.title: An Editorial on My Friend the Football Player
chapters.summary: Leventhal reflects on the stress his involvement placed on his ailing mother, the religious connection he found in his Judaism, and an editorial he wrote in high school about an African American all-American football player from a party who was denied a home because he was Negro, written in support of the spirit of the March on Washington he was too young to attend.

chapters.title: By the End of Summer, the White People Looked Different
chapters.summary: He frames his conversion as the immersion of living in the Black community, where within weeks Black neighbors came to look ordinary and individual rather than different, and where, because white people tried to kill him, by summer's end it was white people who looked different, an acculturation his sociology background helped him name.

chapters.title: He Was the General, We Were the Troops
chapters.summary: Leventhal explains that SCOPE, developed at Hosea Williams's request and approved by the SCLC board in December, targeted 120 counties like a military battle plan drawn by a World War II veteran of Patton's tank brigade, and mentions Jim Colaianni of Ramparts who declined to co-found Rolling Stone with the young Jann Wenner.

chapters.title: They Wanted 2,000, They Got 500
chapters.summary: He describes the 120 chosen counties as winnable swing counties where 30 or 40 percent more Negro voters could oust the racists, contrasts them with all-Black-excluded Lowndes County and its Freedom Organization, and notes that more civil rights workers were killed in 1965 than in 1964, so only 500 of a hoped-for 2,000 volunteers came, almost all white.

chapters.title: The Best Week of Education I Ever Had
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts the SCOPE orientation at Morehouse, recruiting teams sent out during Selma, and an all-star roster of speakers including King, Bayard Rustin, A. Philip Randolph, C. Vann Woodward, John Hope Franklin, Septima Clark, Vernon Jordan, Charles Morgan, Clarence Mitchell, Michael Harrington, and John Doar, who taught them how to file reports.

chapters.title: Muslims and Hindus and Non-Believers
chapters.summary: He remembers King closing his orientation address by naming Jews and Gentiles, Black and white, Muslims and Hindus and non-believers, a far-reaching call to heal divisions of race and religion that Leventhal traces to King's time in India, and a Swedish runner's reminder that the world saw Alabama as being about America itself.

chapters.title: It Freed the Whites Too
chapters.summary: Leventhal notes Alabama's later unanimous pardon of the Scottsboro Boys, white allies like Clifford and Virginia Durr and Aubrey Williams, and his visit to Koinonia Farm near Americus, home of Clarence Jordan and birthplace of Habitat through Millard and Linda Fuller, where he took his rest leave after jail.

chapters.title: A Cow Pasture and a Great Local Leader
chapters.summary: He describes a water hole at Koinonia and a baseball field in a Round Oak cow pasture as his two country firsts, then his assignment to Bibb County and Macon, Georgia, where the SCLC sent volunteers because of strong local leaders William P. Randall and the lawyer Tom Jackson, who became the first Black judge there.

chapters.title: You Go Where I Tell You
chapters.summary: Leventhal tells how Larry Scott Butler's group was reassigned to Barbour County, the home of George Wallace, to raise Helen, and how Hosea Williams ended any debate by declaring he was the general and the volunteers were the troops, with Willie Bolden enforcing that the SCLC, not the white students, ran everything.

chapters.title: Jackie on the Front Lines
chapters.summary: He recounts that SNCC disliked SCOPE workers being told to dress preppy and avoid flaunting interracial couples, and praises Jackie Robinson going at King's request into Birmingham, St. Augustine, and burned-out Albany churches in rural Southwest Georgia, photographed with Wyatt Tee Walker, putting himself in harm's way again and again.

chapters.title: My First Summer Off the Diamond
chapters.summary: Leventhal explains this was to be his first summer without baseball since age ten after an injury-shortened high school career on a team that sent eleven players to Division One and two to the majors, and how, back at UCLA, his letter about playing in the South got him a tryout, while fellow worker Willie Bowens came home with him.

chapters.title: Lots of Willies
chapters.summary: He describes William P. Randall taking the UCLA students to a different Macon church every night for mass meetings, recruiting young people like Willie Bowens from East Macon where Leventhal lived with the Simmons family, and Willie Bowens offering to get him a tryout with the Macon Bombers, which he attended wearing his football jersey.

chapters.title: The Bread Truck Team Bus
chapters.topic: Getting to Round Oak and a First Play
chapters.summary: Leventhal recalls Terrell Simmons, whose bread truck served as the team bus, riding forty miles to a cow-pasture diamond in Round Oak where he first noticed the cow patties, and his first ball in the field bobbling out of his glove before a quick recovery and throw, a close play ruled out that he believed the umpire gave him on purpose as a welcome to the white kid working with Dr. King.

chapters.title: Eleven Times On Base
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts batting without any practice because he did voter registration on weekdays, reaching base eleven of fifteen times mostly on long fouled-off walks, driving in a tying run with a sacrifice fly, and a hit deep to center in Milledgeville that brought a runner home standing up.

chapters.title: Ralph Saved Me That Game
chapters.topic: Errors, a Slugger, and a Reunion
chapters.summary: He describes an error at the integrated Air Force field in Warner Robins, his teammate Ralph Eason hitting a ball clear out of the lights to win the game, and a later Macon Bombers reunion and clinic where a longtime policeman, knowing the only white Bomber must have been a civil rights worker, shared a moment of recognition, at historic Luther Williams Field where Jackie Robinson once played.

chapters.title: You Want Birmingham, or You Want Business
chapters.summary: He recounts being the only one arrested in Macon while Rev. Schiller was the only one beaten, explaining that William P. Randall had pressed the town's leaders to choose business and quiet over the publicity of Birmingham or Selma, with the white citizens council photographing the workers from across the courthouse.

chapters.title: We Have to Start Making Some Numbers
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes Diane Hirsch and Gloria Wise, the stalling of the voting rights bill so Southern legislators could wait the volunteers out, and the decision, after wasting gas on people too afraid to come downtown, to bring registrants directly to the courthouse, where he signed up seven more in twenty minutes.

chapters.title: Nigger Lover and a Smart Aleck
chapters.summary: He tells how, working the bars and pool halls of Broadway, his roommate Ken Long's car was ticketed and an officer, believing Leventhal had made a remark, arrested him as he phoned the office from a barbershop, and how he sat nervous in the back seat over which way the car would turn before it turned right toward the jail.

chapters.title: Chief, What Are Your Officers Doing
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts being out of jail within ten minutes when William P. Randall intervened, filmed crossing to Randall's office by Fred Hoffman, the SCOPE office that later became Phil Walden's Capricorn Records, and Randall barking the police chief into dropping the unknown charge so that the apology spread among the officers, leaving Macon safer than Americus where shots came without warning.

chapters.title: He Hit the Gas Instead of the Brake
chapters.summary: Leventhal tells of his second Macon arrest when William P. Randall's nephew William C., sixteen and eager to impress two Black girls, drove Ken's car without power steering into a parked car in a Black man's yard, leading to arrests for the crash and a knife, and a sixty-dollar fine his father grudgingly paid.

chapters.title: Riflemen at the Church Doors
chapters.summary: He recounts being called to Americus the night after Andrew Whatley was killed, with four women jailed, Dick Gregory leading marches, and Hosea Williams running night marches, sleeping in church pews guarded by two young Black men with rifles, brought in as reinforcements for what Williams meant to make the Selma of the summer.

chapters.title: You Better Take King and Not SNCC
chapters.summary: Leventhal frames nonviolence as the dramatizing of injustice from King's Letter from Birmingham Jail, argues the movement's whole death toll was less than a single day of the Arab Spring, and recounts the Kennedy Library tape that David Garrow misrepresented, in which Kennedy told three Birmingham businessmen their laws were the problem and they should be glad it was King and not the tougher SNCC, sons of bitches he meant as a compliment.

chapters.title: The Kid Who Kept Raising His Hand
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes becoming known for volunteering at every mass meeting, being sent with Rev. Schiller in their suits to tell the media of a press conference at Andrew Whatley's funeral, and his father's silent pride, then his first words, when Leventhal told him he had decided to go South.

chapters.title: But I'm a Baptist and This Is a Baptist Church
chapters.topic: John Lewis at the Church Door
chapters.summary: He recalls going with John Lewis to integrate a Baptist church, where Fire Chief Henderson, tape recorder in hand, refused them and Lewis, a former seminarian, quoted scripture on the equality of all in Christ until the flustered chief ordered their arrest, footage that ran on CBS and the national networks.

chapters.title: We Are Going to Dramatize the Churches
chapters.summary: Leventhal explains that Hosea Williams targeted the First Baptist and First Methodist churches because the SNCC-then-SCOPE worker Lena Turner had written every white church and only those two refused, that the Methodist national body had forbidden exclusion, and praises Sammy Mahone's active Sumter County movement organization today.

chapters.title: You Lead the Group
chapters.topic: The Methodist March and a Segregated Jail
chapters.summary: He recounts Willie Bolden handing him the march to the Methodist church because Bolden could not be arrested, the line met by men with blackjacks, a prayer before arrest, the photograph that ran on front pages from the LA Times to the New York Times, and Sheriff Fred Chappell segregating them in jail against the 1964 Civil Rights Act while firemen fired slingshots and ball bearings at released prisoners.

chapters.title: Too Prominent to Kill
chapters.summary: He tells of Warren Fortson, Jimmy Carter's lawyer and brother of the sitting secretary of state, run out of town for proposing a biracial council, of Red Heffner who left McComb after hosting interracial meetings, and of a Selma family solving an interracial romance by simply declaring their daughter no longer white.

chapters.title: I Could Not Live It
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts being jailed with white men who had thrown glass at Black children and who talked so vilely he covered his ears, and admits that by summer's end, between football, the shootings, and daily fear, he found he could practice nonviolence as a tactic but could not be spiritually nonviolent as King could.

chapters.title: A Guy From Grant High, Down in Americus
chapters.summary: He tells of getting shot at from six feet away, then running into Rich Adler, a Hall of Fame gymnast from his Los Angeles high school who had joined SNCC and been beaten, and Adler's idea to organize the maids working eighty hours for twenty dollars a week by driving them to the Freedom Center.

chapters.title: They Wouldn't Get in the Car
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes driving Fred Hoffman's Citroen, the only one in the state and a marker of outsiders, around scattered Black neighborhoods to recruit maids who feared riding with two white boys lest the shooters miss them and hit the Black passengers, and noticing a pickup truck beginning to follow them.

chapters.title: Bang, Bang, Bang From the Next Truck
chapters.summary: He recounts rolling down the window expecting a thrown brick because his housemate Bill Raul had been beaten with one, only for a pickup to pull alongside and a passenger to fire a gun a few feet away, and finding no bullet holes afterward, leaving him unsure to this day whether the rounds were real or blanks.

chapters.title: The Black Guys Knew to Duck
chapters.topic: A Second Shot and a Lasting Fear
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes being shot at again the next day, hearing the bullet hit the bushes, and diving for cover while the Black men beside him, knowing the shooters wanted the white worker, did not, and explains that whites hated white civil rights workers as traitors and white niggers more than they hated Black activists.

chapters.title: You Can't Leave, We Need You Here
chapters.summary: He recounts staying nearly three extra weeks past the August project end for the September election in Macon after registering over three thousand voters, while everyone else went home, and the candidacy of Rev. Marcus Moon, the first Black to run for Macon office since Reconstruction.

chapters.title: Signed by the Sixth, Watts Blew on the Eleventh
chapters.summary: Leventhal relays Rick Tuttle's argument that SCOPE was critical because it kept pressure on to get the Voting Rights Act signed on August 6th, before the Watts riots blew up on the 11th, since the Southern legislators were stalling to strip the enforcement clause that the 1957 law had lacked.

chapters.title: It's Not 1965, I Have to Calm Myself Down
chapters.summary: He describes the post-traumatic stress that followed him home, the lifelong unease at cars behind him, the campus psychologists at UCLA likening it to Peace Corps readjustment, and his insistence that SNCC has never come to grips with how PTSD shaped their hostility toward King and their sense of unrecognized sacrifice.

chapters.title: The First Person I Saw Was a Black Sheriff
chapters.summary: Leventhal tells of his dread of going to Hayneville, where Jonathan Daniels was killed, dissolving when the first person he saw there was a Black sheriff, and recalls white Macon allies like Betty Adams, the first white woman in the postal department, who hosted the town's first integrated church meeting at her home.

chapters.title: If You're Still Here in 24 Hours, You're Dead
chapters.summary: He recounts election night, taking a call at the SCOPE office from a man whose voice turned from friendly to menacing with a death threat for carrying Negroes to vote, and William P. Randall insisting, against Leventhal's drunken refusal, on driving him to the airport himself the next morning.

chapters.title: Commit a Year of Your Life
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes being among about forty asked to join the SCLC field staff and commit a year, his decision to stay only two weeks for the election since joining would have cost his student deferment, and the August 28th gathering at Abernathy's West Hunter Street Baptist Church where King came to thank them.

chapters.title: He Was Like the Youth Minister
chapters.summary: He recounts King reviewing their tally of registered voters, explaining his controversial 1965 stand against the war so they could defend it, and taking questions in a small group, where Leventhal, not awestruck because he had met his heroes, found King soft-spoken and kind, a famous man at pains to put young people at ease.

chapters.title: Taylor Is a Coward
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts being jailed under a police chief who was a former FBI agent and a psychiatrist tied to Hoover, and charges that Taylor Branch and David Garrow made money exploiting the FBI's acknowledged smear campaign against King, trashed SCOPE, and cut allies like Harris Wofford from their accounts out of ego.

chapters.title: We're Going to Sabotage SCOPE
chapters.summary: He describes Willie Ricks telling him to leave Americus, the Holly Springs meeting of May 1965 where Stokely Carmichael ordered SCOPE sabotaged over Ralph Featherstone's objection, and the late-life moment when Carmichael, beside Louis Farrakhan, told followers never to disparage King because King never attacked back.

chapters.title: It Only Took You 40 Years
chapters.summary: Leventhal tells of Willie Ricks finally befriending him forty-five years later after telling him in 1965 that a Black state trooper's beating had made him hate all white people, likens it to Malcolm X's change after Mecca, and insists the true history of SCOPE includes the 500 who went South and lived with Black families.

chapters.title: Time to Get Over It
chapters.summary: He argues that the criticism of King for not riding the buses ignored that King had children while the riders were single, attributes much SNCC resentment to post-traumatic stress and depression, and praises Sammy Mahone, the former SNCC leader in Americus, as the model of inclusion who embraced both SNCC and SCOPE.

chapters.title: Our Coordinator Is Here
chapters.topic: Named to Lead in the West
chapters.summary: Leventhal recounts returning to a meeting where Gwen Green announced the coordinator had arrived, looking behind him before realizing she meant him, and describes the Western Christian Leadership Conference under Rev. Thomas Kilgore, the SCLC's regional office, and an Operation Breadbasket effort modeled on Jesse Jackson's in Chicago.

chapters.title: Work to Do Where I Live
chapters.summary: He describes the WCLC board and its multi-state western region, Gwen Green fighting as a woman to lead it, his own modest organizing in Northern and Southern California, the white Macon critics who asked whether LA had no problems of its own, and King's posthumous Look magazine appeal that drew him toward the Poor People's Campaign.

chapters.title: They're Going to Bomb My Place
chapters.summary: Leventhal tells how his father threatened to cut him off if he joined the Poor People's Campaign, and how, after Black children visited his Santa Monica apartment, his landlord relayed a bomb threat, leading him, on his rabbi's and the police's advice, to buy a shotgun for protection.

chapters.title: Drop the Gun, They Counted to One
chapters.summary: He recounts calling the ex-cop landlord to verify the threat, the landlord reporting him as menacing with a shotgun, and city attorney Herbert Lapine and two officers confronting him in his alley with guns drawn, counting down as he insisted the open shotgun could not fire before he dropped it and they seized it without taking his report.

chapters.title: Don't Touch Me, You Can Arrest Me
chapters.summary: Leventhal describes returning repeatedly for a property receipt until officers arrested him in the phone booth for disturbing the peace, twisting his arm, and how outside the booking room he knocked an officer down before four others pulled him off and slammed his head on the ground until he passed out, then declined a deal to leave town.

chapters.title: Eight of Ten Said I Wasn't Crazy
chapters.summary: He recounts a threatening-phone-call charge and a psychiatric hold meant to lead to commitment, the jail psychiatrist Ronald Markman clearing him as sane, a second bogus battery arrest and 112 days held while chief psychiatrist Marcus Crahan wrote a damning report, and how, needing two psychiatrists to commit him, the system sent him to ten, the last ones quietly warning he was being railroaded as Herbert Lapine vowed to put him away for life.

chapters.title: Two Hundred and Twelve Days Later
chapters.summary: He recounts a Nation of Islam inmate cutting his eye with a razor-bladed bar of soap, the deputies warming to him afterward, and how, with no psychiatrists left to enable a commitment, the court commuted his six-month sentence to time served on the condition of outpatient counseling, a deal his father urged him to take.

chapters.title: Think What They Can Do to a Poor Black Person
chapters.topic: Qualified Immunity and the Innocence Project
chapters.summary: Leventhal closes by signing a stipulation of probable cause that left him depressed and lifelong distrustful of the justice system, and connects his ordeal to today's arguable probable cause and qualified immunity, an Iowa civil rights case he took toward the Supreme Court, and the overload of the Innocence Project.

loc_video.caption: William S. Leventhal oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in El Segundo, California, 2013 April 13
